lane: A7 (A-g resize + A-h clones / hard links / sparse / xattr / sealed)
environment: qemu/KVM macOS Sequoia recovery (BaseSystem), apfs.kext; OSX-KVM snap3 USB config (ICH9-LPC.disable_s3=1, ich9-EHCI + nec-usb-xhci msi=off); headless vncdotool drive
image: S.A.K.-generated APFS container (format 64 MiB -> insert data.bin -> clone data.bin -> hard link data.bin -> in-chunk grow to 100 MiB), attached as a raw IDE drive; host apfsck -cw clean (exit 0)
kernel_mount: macOS kernel auto-mounted the S.A.K. container's volume SAKA7 at /Volumes/SAKA7 (the grown 100 MiB geometry parsed + mounted)

clone.proof: md5(/Volumes/SAKA7/clone.bin) == md5(/Volumes/SAKA7/data.bin) == daf7118243ce01cd672b57338c3d0089; clone is inode 17 (its own inode, nlink 1) with its own file-extent records pointing at the source's physical blocks (extent-ref refcount 2)
clone.kernel_caught_bug: the earlier shared-private-id clone (clone inode reused the source's dstream id with no own extents) read as ALL ZEROS on the macOS kernel -- the kernel keys file-extents by the inode's OWN object id, not private_id. apfsck and the S.A.K. reader both accepted it; only the kernel byte-read caught it. Fixed: clone emits its own extents + extent-ref refcnt rises to 2.

hard_link.proof: data.bin and link.bin are both inode 16, nlink 2, md5 == daf7118243ce01cd672b57338c3d0089 (kernel resolved the two names to one inode + identical bytes)

resize_in_chunk_grow.proof: diskutil info disk1 -> Disk Size 104.9 MB (104857600 Bytes) = the grown 100 MiB; fsck_apfs -ln: '** The volume /dev/rdisk1s1 ... appears to be OK.' + '** The container /dev/disk1 appears to be OK.'

sparse.proof: earlier same-session pass on the combined image: ls size 65536 (logical), tail bytes read as zeros (kernel zero-fills the hole), inode 17 nlink 1

xattr_acl.certified: host-apfsck + unit; kernel mount tolerated (xattr CLI absent in the recovery environment, not separately read back)

sealed_volume_policy.certified: unit (fail-closed gate; not a mountable artifact)

fsck_apfs: ** The volume /dev/rdisk1s1 with UUID CAD7B919-B378-44A3-9A88-0C9CAC64C6A1 appears to be OK. ** The container /dev/disk1 appears to be OK.
